title: Butter on Cotton
timeEstimate: 10–25 minutes; longer if set in or hot-dried
stainChemistry: Butter is an oil/fat-family stain. Oil-based stains require a surfactant (liquid laundry detergent) to emulsify and lift the fat from the fiber; surfactants surround the soil and suspend it in water so it rinses away. Cotton is cellulose: white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering, while colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before any escalation.
whyThisWorks: A small amount of liquid laundry detergent applied directly to the stain provides surfactant action to break up butterfat. Cool or lukewarm water (per care label) reduces risk of setting any small protein fraction. Air-drying and inspecting before machine heat prevents residual oil from being baked into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Most butter on cotton is a kitchen / breakfast stain on a washable garment. Fresh butter responds quickly to detergent pretreat plus care-label laundering. Stains that have already been through a hot wash or dryer are harder and may need a professional dry cleaner.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm fiber, color, and care-label instructions. Dry-clean-only or structured pieces escalate to the professional path. Note dye stability for colored cotton.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Reject too-soiled or non-colorfast garments per care-label guidance. Do not proceed if the care label disallows wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Use a dull edge to gently lift any solid butter off the surface. Do not rub or press into the fiber.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Minimal mechanical action only. Aggressive rubbing can spread the stain or damage the fiber finish.

spottingProtocol.instruction: On colored cotton, dab a small amount of liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam using a clean white cloth. Press for one minute and check the cloth for color transfer.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Stop if dye transfers to the white cloth. Colored or unstable-dye garments escalate to the professional path.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent directly to the stain. Work it in lightly with a fingertip or soft cloth.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Brief dwell per product label, then rinse
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Follow the detergent product label. Do not mix cleaning agents. Use PPE and ventilation if any concentrate is involved.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder using the temperature setting permitted by the care label; favor cool or lukewarm when uncertain. Rinse thoroughly.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Do not exceed the temperature limit on the care label.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Air-dry the garment and inspect for residual oil before any dryer heat. If oil remains, repeat the surfactant pretreat and laundering step. Do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Heat applied to residual oil sets the stain into the fiber.

steps: 1. Identify fiber, dye stability, and care-label limits before any wet treatment.
2. Lift excess oil with a dull edge; minimal mechanical action.
3. Apply surfactant pretreat appropriate to the oil family; rinse and assess.
4. Launder per care-label temperature limit; do not exceed.

5. Air-dry and inspect before any heat. If escalation is warranted, apply controlled oxidizer chemistry only when the care label permits and colorfastness has been confirmed, per product SDS and with PPE and ventilation.
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cool to lukewarm per care label; no heat on residual oil
warnings: Do not exceed care-label temperature limits
Confirm colorfastness via hidden-area test before any escalation
Pro chemistry beyond consumer detergent requires SDS-driven PPE and ventilation

homeSolutions.instruction: Check the care label. If it says dry-clean-only, or the garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage, stop and take it to a professional dry cleaner. For washable cotton, continue.
Use a dull butter knife or spoon edge to gently lift any solid butter off the fabric. Do not rub — that pushes the oil deeper into the fiber.

On colored cotton, dab a little liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam with a clean white cloth. Press one minute. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take to a professional. White cotton can skip this step.
Put a small amount of mild liquid laundry detergent directly on the stain. Work it in gently with your fingertip or a soft cloth — do not scrub.
Wash the garment using the temperature your care label allows. If you are unsure, use cool or lukewarm water. Rinse thoroughly.

homeSolutions.agent: Air-Dry and Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Lay the garment flat or hang to air-dry and look closely for any remaining oily mark before putting it in the dryer. If a stain remains, repeat steps 4 and 5 once. Do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone — heat sets residual oil into cotton.

If the stain is still there after two careful passes, or if it has already been through a hot wash or dryer, take it to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them: "Butter on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with liquid laundry detergent and a care-label wash, air-dried."

steps: 1. Read care label; escalate dry-clean-only / structured / vintage to a pro.
2. Lift excess butter gently with a dull edge — do not rub.
3. On colored cotton, hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent.
4. Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent to the stain.
5. Launder per care label (favor cool / lukewarm if uncertain).
6. Air-dry and inspect for residual oil before any dryer heat.
7. Repeat once if needed; escalate if it doesn't clear or was heat-set.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the care label is dry-clean-only, the garment is structured / lined / embellished, the hidden-area test shows color transfer, the stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer, or two conservative passes do not clear it. In any of those cases, escalate to a professional dry cleaner.
warnings: Do not rub — pushes oil into the fiber
Do not machine-dry residual oil — heat sets the stain
Pro-strength oxidizers, solvents, and bleaches are not Home steps

neverDo: Never machine-dry a garment with residual butter — heat sets oil into the fiber
Never rub or scrub the stain aggressively
Never use chlorine bleach on colored cotton
Never apply professional-strength oxidizers, solvents, or acids in the Home path
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is cellulose; white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering
Colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before escalation
Aged or hot-dried oil residue becomes harder to remove and may need a professional dry cleaner
chemicalBoundaries: Home path: mild liquid laundry detergent and water only
No peroxide, oxygen bleach, or chlorine bleach as a default Home step
Pro path may use controlled oxidizer chemistry only when care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and SDS-driven PPE and ventilation are in place
homeAllowed: Cool or lukewarm water per care label
Clean white cloth and dull edge for mechanical lift
Air-drying and visual inspection before any heat
proOnly: Concentrated surfactants, solvents, and oxidizers used in a controlled professional environment with PPE and ventilation per product SDS
testFirst: Hidden-area colorfastness test (white cloth on an inside seam) before detergent dwell or any escalation on colored cotton
flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse thoroughly after each detergent application
Air-dry and inspect before any dryer heat
escalationTriggers: Care label is dry-clean-only or restricts wet treatment
Garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage / high-value
Stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer
Two conservative passes do not clear the stain
ppeVentilation: Follow the product label for any concentrate
Gloves and eye protection for any pro-strength chemistry
Ventilate the work area; keep cleaning agents away from children and pets
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home users use only mild liquid laundry detergent and care-label laundering. Pro chemistry (concentrated oxidizers, solvents) is hidden from the Home path and requires SDS-driven PPE/ventilation in a controlled environment.

materialWarnings: Inspect for residual oil before any dryer heat
Colored cotton: hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent dwell
Aged or hot-dried oil is harder to remove and may need a professional dry cleaner

escalation.when: Dry-clean-only, structured, or vintage cotton; colored cotton that fails a hidden-area test; stain that has been through a hot wash or dryer; or no improvement after two conservative passes.
escalation.whatToTell: "Butter on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with liquid laundry detergent and a care-label wash, air-dried. [Hot wash or dryer applied? — yes / no]."

contextQuestions: Is the cotton white or colored / dyed?
How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, or older?
Has the garment been through a hot wash or dryer since the spill?
Is the garment dry-clean-only, structured, or embellished?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh butter on washable cotton typically clears with conservative surfactant pretreat and a care-label wash. Hot-dried or aged residue is meaningfully harder and should be escalated to a professional dry cleaner rather than treated with stronger Home chemistry.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Oil-family classification; conservative unknown-stain fallback (cool water, liquid detergent, rinse, air-dry); blot rather than rub; do not iron or dry stained fabric until removal is confirmed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Oily soils require surfactant action; detergents are surfactants that suspend fatty soils in water; test new detergents on inconspicuous areas; follow product labels and PPE/ventilation for concentrates; clean per care label or reject garments that are not appropriate for the process.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Cotton fiber tolerances; white cotton tolerant, colored cotton requires lower heat and no chlorine bleach or strong peroxide; lipase/fat use cases respond to surfactant action; absolute safety gates including no cleaner-mixing.
